nativeRange.habitat: Found on tropical coral reefs from 1 to 30 metres depth, where it characteristically chips cavities into the centres of live branching corals — particularly Acropora and Pocillopora — and uses these excavated chambers as burrows. The species ranges across the central Indo-Pacific, reaching into southern China and northern Australia.

decorPreferences: deep live-rock rubble or branching coral skeletons that allow a self-chosen burrow
rockwork securely stacked so the shrimp cannot collapse it while excavating
acrylic tank strongly preferred over thin glass — smasher dactyl strikes have been reported to crack aquarium glass
tightly-fitting lid (smasher mantis are known to climb and jump when stressed)
sand/rubble substrate for digging and burrow extension

diet.notes: The Orange Spot Mantis Shrimp is a strict carnivore and a smasher-type predator that kills hard-shelled prey — snails, hermit crabs, small crabs, and bivalves — with high-velocity strikes from its calcified dactyl club. In captivity it readily accepts frozen mysis, krill, chopped clam, shrimp, scallop, and squid, and many individuals can be trained to take pellets and sinking carnivore foods. Adults do well fed every two to three days; juveniles can be fed more frequently. Offering occasional live snails or hermit crabs is commonly advised to give the dactyl club natural wear and exercise.

goodWith: kept species-only — no permanent tankmates recommended
avoidWith: all snails, hermit crabs, ornamental shrimp, and small crabs (treated as food)
small fish (gobies, blennies, cardinalfish — readily taken)
other mantis shrimp (conspecific aggression is severe)
delicate live corals in the immediate burrow zone (will chip cavities into branching SPS)
any system recently treated with copper-based medications

summary: The Orange Spot Mantis Shrimp (Gonodactylaceus ternatensis) is a compact Indo-Pacific smasher species reaching 7 to 12 cm, recognized by its iridescent green body, red inter-segmental stripes, and the bright orange meral spot that gives it its name.
careNotes: The Orange Spot Mantis Shrimp is a genuine smasher stomatopod — a group named for the calcified dactyl club they use to smash hard-shelled prey — and that club can strike with enough force to crack thin aquarium glass. An acrylic tank or thick low-iron glass is widely recommended for this reason. The species is best kept as a single occupant in a species-only system; it is widely regarded as a relentless predator of snails, hermit crabs, ornamental shrimp, and small fish, and is broadly considered highly territorial toward other mantis shrimp. Provide deep live rock or coral rubble that allows it to excavate or occupy a fixed burrow. In nature this species chips cavities directly into branching corals, so any live SPS corals (fast-growing stony corals such as Acropora) placed close to the burrow are at risk. The species is generally regarded as intermediate in care, the main challenges being its sensitivity to water quality — the Orange Spot is anecdotally more parameter-sensitive than the Peacock Mantis, and allowing the tank to cycle for around six weeks before introduction is commonly advised — and the handler-safety considerations rather than feeding difficulty. Like all marine crustaceans it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications. Handle the tank with great caution: dactyl strikes have been known to draw blood and damage fingers, and using siphon tubes, magnetic algae scrapers, and acrylic shields when servicing the aquarium is strongly advised.
breedingNotes: The Orange Spot Mantis Shrimp is not regularly bred in home aquariums, and all mantis shrimp available in the trade are essentially wild-caught. In the wild, female stomatopods carry egg masses inside the burrow and brood them until hatching; the larvae are long-lived plankton and would require a dedicated planktonic-rearing system to survive in captivity. Captive rearing of Gonodactylaceus species has not been documented in the hobby.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Stated origin (Indo-Pacific) and target water parameters (74–78°F, pH 8.2–8.4, 35 ppt ≈ SG 1.026, NO3 <20 ppm). Product description was otherwise marked 'coming soon', so husbandry detail came from secondaries.

additional.name: Blue Zoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Designated Reef2Reef Wiki secondary returned HTTP 403; swapped to Blue Zoo Aquatics. Confirmed adult size ~5 in (~12.7 cm), minimum tank 24 gal (~90 L), Indo-Pacific origin, aggressive temperament, carnivorous diet (live food and frozen acceptable), reef-incompatible, species-only setup recommended, and acrylic-tank advisory (smashers can crack glass).

additional.name: Lizard Island Field Guide (Australian Museum)
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority: family Gonodactylidae, order Stomatopoda. Wild size 0.8–12 cm (largest goniodactylid). Documented behaviour of chipping cavities into branching coral. Coloration: dark green with red inter-segmental stripes plus the diagnostic bright orange meral spot; sexually dimorphic antennal scales (blue in males, orange/yellow in females).

additional.name: ReefCasa — The Ultimate Mantis Shrimp Pet Guide
additional.notes: Genus-level husbandry: target SG ~1.025, feed every ~3 days, captive lifespan up to ~10 years, generally hardy once parameters are stable, and all hobby specimens are wild-caught (no captive breeding established).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Gonodactylaceus
additional.notes: Used only to corroborate taxonomic placement (Gonodactylidae, Stomatopoda) and species authorship (de Man, 1902). Not used for husbandry parameters.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Adults are generally reported to molt every one to two months; juveniles molt more frequently. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium, and iodine support successful molts. The shrimp typically retreats deep into its burrow before and after a molt and is particularly vulnerable for a few days while the new dactyl club hardens — disturbance should be minimised during this window.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Gonodactylaceus ternatensis has not been documented in the hobby; all trade specimens are wild-caught. Females brood eggs inside the burrow in the wild, and the larvae are planktonic — rearing would require a dedicated planktonic system.
